name: CCC Camp NM-87 Company 1102 - Sierra National Forest
description: Company 1102 established Camp NM-87 near the Sespe Creek watershed in 1935, where enrollees undertook massive reforestation efforts, planting millions of pine and fir seedlings under direction of Forest Service supervisors. The camp operated with a full complement of barracks, mess hall, infirmary, and equipment sheds arranged along a level bench above the seasonal floodplain. A severe winter storm in 1935 damaged several barracks buildings, requiring the company to spend weeks on emergency reconstruction before resuming project work. Access requires a short drive on a gravel forest road with the former camp situated on a terrace above the creek. Detectorists report finding 1930s coinage, CCC belt hardware, brass uniform buttons, and fragments of enamelware mess equipment.
source: CCC Legacy Foundation Archive

name: CCC Camp DG-76 Company SP-26 - Sierra National Forest
description: Established in 1935 along the Big Creek drainage, Camp DG-76 housed Company SP-26 during the height of New Deal conservation efforts in the Sierra National Forest district. The enrollees constructed an extensive network of forest roads and bridges that still serve the area today while also maintaining camp infrastructure and conducting daily regimented fitness activities. The company's foreman was a veteran of the Spanish-American War whose military discipline shaped the unit into one of the region's most productive CCC companies. A Forest Service interpretive sign near the original site commemorates CCC contributions, accessible via a short walk from the main trailhead. Detectorists report finding 1930s coinage, CCC belt hardware, brass uniform buttons, and fragments of enamelware mess equipment.
source: CCC Legacy Foundation Archive

name: CCC Camp F-94 Company SP-49 - Sierra National Forest
description: Company SP-49 operated Camp F-94 from 1933-1939 as part of the nationwide CCC initiative bringing unemployed young men to public lands for conservation work. Situated near the Mount San Jacinto slopes, the company conducted tree disease surveys and timber stand improvement operations and contributed substantially to the forest's infrastructure legacy. The camp served as a staging area for crews fighting one of the largest fire seasons in the district's history during the drought years of the late 1930s. The site lies within publicly accessible National Forest land reachable by forest road, though no formal facilities mark the camp location. Detectorists report finding 1930s coinage, CCC belt hardware, brass uniform buttons, and fragments of enamelware mess equipment.
source: USFS Oral History Collection, Sierra National Forest

name: CCC Camp NM-30 Company 1284 - Sierra National Forest
description: Company 1284 operated Camp NM-30 from 1933-1942 as part of the nationwide CCC initiative bringing unemployed young men to public lands for conservation work. Situated near the Kings River drainage, the company constructed telephone lines and communication infrastructure across remote terrain and contributed substantially to the forest's infrastructure legacy. Oral histories collected by the Forest Service in the 1970s from former enrollees described the camp as having an unusually strong sense of camaraderie. The former camp grounds lie along a maintained forest road that passes through the drainage, easily accessible by passenger vehicle in summer months. Known recoveries include pre-Depression coinage, CCC-issue collar discs, iron ax wedges, and hand-forged hardware from the barracks area.
source: USFS Sierra National Forest Supervisor's Annual Reports

name: CCC Camp P-60 Company 1292 - Sierra National Forest
description: Established in 1935 along the San Gabriel Canyon, Camp P-60 housed Company 1292 during the height of New Deal conservation efforts in the Sierra National Forest district. source: USFS Sierra National Forest Historical Records

name: CCC Camp G-83 Company 1864 - Sierra National Forest
description: From 1935, Company 1864 called Camp G-83 home, working across the Mount San Jacinto slopes region of the Sierra National Forest. The dump pits have produced 1930s coins, glass insulators from telephone lines, cast iron cookware fragments, and CCC tokens.
source: Rocky Mountain Region USFS CCC Documentation

name: CCC Camp G-13 Company 3013 - Hiawatha National Forest
description: From 1934, Company 3013 called Camp G-13 home, working across the Whitefish Bay shoreline region of the Hiawatha National Forest. The company constructed a ranger station complex and administrative buildings, transforming previously inaccessible terrain into a managed and recreationally productive forest. Oral histories collected by the Forest Service in the 1970s from former enrollees described the camp as having an unusually strong sense of camaraderie. The camp site is within a designated Recreation Area, accessible from the main highway via a gravel spur road. Detectorists working the site have recovered wheat pennies, CCC belt buckles, military-style uniform buttons, and fragments of hand tools.
source: CCC Enrollment Records, National Personnel Records Center

name: CCC Camp P-1 Company 1213 - Hiawatha National Forest
description: Company 1213 established Camp P-1 near the Lake Superior corridor in 1935, where enrollees fought multiple large forest fires in summer months and conducted fire prevention work under direction of Forest Service supervisors. The camp operated with a full complement of barracks, mess hall, infirmary, and equipment sheds arranged along a level bench above the seasonal floodplain. Several of the stone walls built by this company have been placed on the National Register of Historic Places as examples of New Deal craftsmanship. The former camp grounds lie along a maintained forest road that passes through the drainage, easily accessible by passenger vehicle in summer months. The site has produced wheat cents, buffalo nickels, CCC company buttons, wire-handled buckets, and work tool fragments.
source: USFS MI Regional Office CCC Files

name: CCC Camp DG-62 Company 1532 - Chequamegon National Forest
description: Established in 1935 along the Namekagon River corridor, Camp DG-62 housed Company 1532 during the height of New Deal conservation efforts in the Chequamegon National Forest district. The enrollees constructed a ranger station complex and administrative buildings while also maintaining camp infrastructure and conducting daily regimented fitness activities. An outbreak of influenza swept through the camp in 1938, temporarily halting all project work for nearly three weeks. A Forest Service interpretive sign near the original site commemorates CCC contributions, accessible via a short walk from the main trailhead. Metal detecting has produced Roosevelt-era coins, CCC identification tags, military surplus buckles, and mess hall serving spoons.
source: National Archives CCC Record Group 35

name: CCC Camp BH-83 Company 2545 - Chequamegon National Forest
description: Company 2545 established Camp BH-83 near the Namekagon River corridor in 1935, where enrollees undertook massive reforestation efforts, planting millions of pine and fir seedlings under direction of Forest Service supervisors. The camp operated with a full complement of barracks, mess hall, infirmary, and equipment sheds arranged along a level bench above the seasonal floodplain. The company's foreman was a veteran of the Spanish-American War whose military discipline shaped the unit into one of the region's most productive CCC companies. The location is accessible year-round on a paved forest highway with the approximate camp area identified by local forest historians. The dump pits have produced 1930s coins, glass insulators from telephone lines, cast iron cookware fragments, and CCC tokens.
source: Rocky Mountain Region USFS CCC Documentation
